◆

The cost of being alive, mapped

Real data · India · dashboard

Entropy is framed as a lever on lifespan. But across India, how long you live is set first by where you are born. This is real government life-expectancy data, by state. Filter by region, or tap a state to compare it.

Lens Life expectancy at birth, both sexes (SRS).

A child born in Kerala can expect to live about a decade longer than one born in Chhattisgarh, before a single lifestyle choice is made. Longevity is metabolic, but it is first healthcare, water, income and air, the baseline any cost of being alive sits on top of.

Region

Life expectancy at birth, both sexes. Source: SRS Abridged Life Tables, Government of India. Boundaries simplified for the web; Telangana is shown within Andhra Pradesh. Grey states are not separately reported here.

A body is a signal. The whole game is reading it without fooling yourself, and saying so when you cannot.the method, in the open ↓
01

Signal

Hard problem · method shown

Any optical metabolic device lives or dies on one thing: holding a clean pulse signal while the body moves. Motion artifact rejection on PPG is a genuine open problem. Here's a real pipeline, bandpass, adaptive noise cancellation, spectral tracking, working live. Hit it with motion and watch it recover.

PPG recovery · oscilloscopestable
raw (sensor) recovered (pipeline) motion reference
True pulse·· bpm
Recovered·· bpm
Abs error·· bpm
Est. energy*·· ≈ kcal/min

*Energy is a rough heart-rate proxy, not a calibrated measurement. The honest version of this number needs lab calorimetry, which no watch has.

What you're looking at

The grey trace is a synthesised photoplethysmogram, a cardiac fundamental, its harmonics, and a slow respiratory baseline. Inject motion and a correlated artifact (the blue reference) swamps it, exactly as a wrist or temple sensor sees during movement.

The gold trace is the recovered signal: a Butterworth bandpass isolates the pulse band, a normalised‑LMS adaptive filter subtracts the motion reference, and an FFT peak‑tracker reads instantaneous rate. The energy figure is a transparent heart‑rate→VO₂ proxy, shown only to illustrate the downstream step.

Honest scope: the algorithm is real and running in your browser; the signal is synthetic so the mechanism stays visible and falsifiable. The same class of pipeline is then run on real wrist PPG during exercise in Module 05, scored against ECG with Bland‑Altman agreement rather than pooled correlation. No real‑subject accuracy is claimed on this panel; the measured numbers live there.

Why it matters to Temple

This is Temple's core engineering problem in miniature. The temple site genuinely sits over the superficial temporal artery, a real signal‑quality advantage. But "clean signal in motion" is where these devices actually fail, and it's the right place to prove competence.

02

Lifespan

A kernel · oversold as a law

Temple's longevity pitch, as I read it, is that a lower cost of being alive at rest tends to go with a longer life across the animal world. That is the century‑old rate‑of‑living theory. So I tested it on the real thing: 547 species from the AnAge database, with metabolic rate, body mass and maximum lifespan. The toggle does the one thing that decides the argument.

AnAge · metabolic rate vs lifespan r = ··
mammals birds reptiles amphibians fish
X axismass‑specific metabolic rate

Raw view: looks like Temple's right

Plot mass‑specific metabolic rate against lifespan and the pattern is real and strong: r = −0.47. Faster‑burning animals die younger. If you stop here, as the marketing does, the rate‑of‑living law looks settled.

But there's a confound hiding in plain sight: body size. Big animals burn slower per gram and live longer for unrelated reasons. So the raw correlation is mostly size wearing a metabolism costume. Switch the toggle to strip size out.

Data: AnAge (Human Ageing Genomic Resources, 2023 build), species with all three measures present. I computed the statistics for this project; the analysis script is reproducible. Hover any point.

Verdict

A kernel of truth, oversold. Once body mass is held constant the effect nearly vanishes, and in birds it disappears entirely. Birds run ~2× the metabolism of same‑size mammals yet live ~2× longer, flatly breaking the "law." Temple's framing is directionally evocative but not a cross‑animal rule.

03

The sensing site

Physiology · a case, not a proof

Why read at the temple at all? The data in Module 05 gave a hint: accuracy lived or died on how much the sensor moved with the body. The temple sits over a vessel with two real advantages, and some real problems. Here is the honest case for the site.

Layers
sensorsuperficial temporal arterylive read
Pulse 72 bpm
01

Central, not peripheral

The superficial temporal artery is a terminal branch of the external carotid: it supplies the scalp and face, sitting close to central circulation but not feeding brain tissue, which is the internal carotid's job. Unlike a fingertip, it is not shut down first when the body diverts blood, so the pulse stays present.

02

Resists the cold-hands problem

Forehead and temple vessels hold up under the peripheral vasoconstriction that kills finger and wrist signals in cold or stress. This is exactly why clinics put reflectance oximeters on the forehead for low-perfusion patients.

03

The honest problems

It is not free. Facial and jaw muscles, eyewear shift, sweat, and hair all inject motion, and the tissue is thin. The deeper caveat is interpretive: because this vessel feeds the scalp, not the brain, neurologist Dr. Sudhir Kumar publicly argued the signal cannot stand in for cerebral blood flow and pointed to the neck's internal carotid instead. Temple's own site is careful too, claiming only that the artery sits close to the central circulatory system. Public temporal‑PPG datasets barely exist, so the site stays a physiological case, not a measured result.

04

Gravity

Real number · big leap

The idea seeding Temple's sister venture: standing upright drains blood from the brain, and over a lifetime that drives ageing. The acute number is real. The question is whether you can ride it all the way to "decades of decline." Tilt the body and see what the physiology actually does.

Hydrostatic load on the brain standing · 90°
Does the dip pile up over a lifetime?age 20 to 80

Standing costs the brain a real but buffered dip. The acute fall is genuine physiology. Autoregulation absorbs the bulk of it within seconds, so the lifetime curve above stays flat rather than sliding into decline. That gap, between a buffered dip and lifelong damage, is the part of the gravity-ageing idea the evidence does not yet cross.

Body posturestanding, 90°
Height175 cm
Arterial elasticityhealthy
Acute flow change
−14%
The brief drop the moment you stand. Driven by the column of blood the heart must lift to the head.
What the brain holds
−3%
After autoregulation defends flow. Seconds later this is most of what is left.

The honest physiology

Tilt from lying to standing and cerebral blood‑flow velocity does drop. Published transcranial‑Doppler work puts the acute fall around 10 to 20%, and Continue's own hypothesis page cites a 10% reduction (some press coverage rounded this up to 17%). So the headline figure isn't invented.

But the brain has cerebral autoregulation (the grey plateau): across a wide pressure range it actively defends near‑constant flow. The acute dip is largely buffered and transient, not a permanent deficit. That's the gap the hypothesis has to cross, and the evidence doesn't carry it across.

Switch to decades: the leap from a buffered, seconds‑long dip to lifelong neurodegeneration is unsupported. Ageing is multifactorial (the hallmarks of ageing), and microgravity cuts the other way, in spaceflight, fluid shifts toward the head and is linked to raised intracranial pressure (SANS), not protection. Curves here are representative of established physiology, not new measurements.

Verdict

The number is real; the mechanism is a leap. Postural CBF changes are well documented and worth measuring. Promoting them to a primary driver of ageing is a hypothesis far ahead of its evidence, interesting to study, not yet a fact to sell.

05

Validation lab

Real data · ECG ground truth

Module 01 showed the method on a synthetic signal. This is the same class of pipeline run on real wrist PPG recorded during exercise, with a chest ECG as the reference heart rate. The question is the one every wearable has to answer honestly: not "does it correlate," but "by how many beats is it wrong, and where."

The one-line read: on steady cycling the wrist tracker lands within a few beats of the ECG truth. When the arm-swing cadence sits right on the heart rate, walking and running, it can be off by twenty or more. Good in some motion, not solved, and honest about which.

Source PhysioNet · Wrist PPG During Exercise (Jarchi & Casson 2017) · 8 subjects · walk / run / cycle · 256 Hz

Naive estimator·· bpm MAEsingle FFT peak per window
Motion-robust·· bpm MAEaccel-suppressed + tracked
Cycling, robust·· bpm MAEwhere motion is separable
Reliable windows··reference-grade subset
Estimated vs ECG heart rate · one record over time
ECG truth naive PPG motion-robust PPG
Bland-Altman · agreement with ECG
x: mean of the two · y: estimate minus ECG

● walking● running● cycling

Mean error by activity
naive robust

Cycling is the clean win: the wrist is steady, the heart rate is high, and the step-free motion sits far from the pulse in frequency, so the tracker locks on to within a few beats. Walking and running put the arm-swing cadence right on top of the heart rate, which is exactly where this stays a genuinely open problem.

The reference itself breaks under motion. Admitting that is the rigor.

A surprising finding fell out of the data: during walking and running, the chest ECG, the supposed gold standard, is itself corrupted by motion. Its beat-to-beat heart rate scatters by 4 to 5 bpm inside a single window, against 2.2 bpm at rest on the bike. So a chunk of the "error" on foot is the yardstick moving, not just the estimate. Every number above is computed only on the ·· of windows where the ECG reference is trustworthy. Reporting on a yardstick you have not checked is how wearable accuracy gets oversold.

Reference quality by activity

Within-window scatter of the ECG heart rate. Lower is a more trustworthy reference. Cycling is reference-grade; on-foot is not.

Robust error by subject

Same method, held-out people, wildly different accuracy. This generalization spread is why a number from one wrist means little until it is tested leave-one-subject-out.

Why it matters to Temple

This is the test Temple's "Entropy tracked the cart at r = 0.93" needs to survive: not a correlation, but agreement limits, on held-out people, with a reference you have audited. The honest result here also argues for Temple's own thesis. Accuracy collapsed exactly when the sensing site (the wrist) was flailing. A site that moves less with the body, like the temple over the superficial temporal artery, is a reasonable bet to push these limits in. That is a hypothesis to test, which is the next section, not a thing to assert.

06

Why r = 0.93 doesn't mean what it sounds like

One statistic, easy to win

Temple's headline is a correlation: Entropy r = 0.93 against a metabolic cart, heart rate 0.55. Correlation measures whether two things rise and fall together. It does not measure whether they agree. You can be highly correlated and consistently, badly wrong. Drag the controls and watch a near-perfect r sit on top of an unusable measurement.

Points are a synthetic measurement against a reference. Only the scatter moves the correlation. Gain and offset, the things that make a device read wrong, barely touch r at all.

Pearson r··
Bias··
95% limits··
Lin's CCC··
agreement and correlation match here
measurement vs referencedashed: perfect agreement
Bland-Altmanband: 95% limits
The honest reading

Two separate problems hide inside a high r. First, agreement: r rewards any straight line, but a measurement only agrees when that line has slope 1 and intercept 0. Second, the baseline: comparing an optimized composite (0.93) against a single raw variable (0.55) is not a fair fight. The right comparison for heart rate is a properly calibrated heart-rate-plus-motion model, not raw beats. None of this says Entropy is wrong. It says one correlation cannot carry the claim, and the statistics that can, limits of agreement, concordance, test-retest, leave-one-subject-out, are exactly what an independent check should ask for.

07

An open demand index

An open, checkable index

Temple distils demand into one private number. Here is the opposite: a transparent demand index with every term and weight on the table, computable by anyone from signals a watch already has. It is not a copy of Entropy and makes no claim to be. It is what a one-number demand score looks like when the method is open to inspection.

The whole method, nothing hidden
demand = 250 · clip(
  0.55 · hr_reserve
  + 0.27 · sympathetic
  + 0.18 · motion
)
hr_reserve = (HR minus resting) / (max minus resting). Where the heart sits in its own range. This is the term validated in Module 05.
sympathetic = 1 minus normalized HRV. Falling beat-to-beat variability is a stand-in for sympathetic drive.
motion = normalized accelerometer power. Mechanical work the body is doing right now.
··
Demand index · 1 to 250
heart
sympath.
motion
What is validated

The heart-rate term is checked against ECG in Module 05, on real exercise data, with agreement limits, not just a correlation. On cycling it lands within about 2.5 bpm.

What is not

The map from this index to true metabolic rate in kcal needs indirect calorimetry, which this public dataset does not contain. So this is an honest demand index, not a calibrated calorimeter.

That gap is the whole point: a calorimeter-grade claim, like Entropy's, has to be earned with calorimeter-grade validation, in the open.

08

A day, in Entropy

Illustrative model, not device data

Temple frames Entropy as one number that tracks the cost of being alive across a whole day, moved by sleep, caffeine, training and rest. This is that idea made playable. Toggle the things a day is made of and watch the curve, the peak ceiling and the resting floor recompute. The numbers come from a transparent model below, not from any sensor.

The day

Tap to add or remove. Some inputs lift the curve, sleep and stillness pull it down.

24-hour Entropy index, 1 to 250
--
Entropy at now
Time above 150
0h
What is shaping the curve
Entropy Maxima
120
ceiling
The highest the body reaches under load. Push this up.
Entropy Minima
45
floor
The lowest resting cost across the day. Bring this down.
Daily average
--
Mean cost across 24 hours, the area under the curve.

How the curve is built: a circadian baseline runs low in deep night and rises through the day. Each input adds a shaped contribution over the hours it touches, a sharp spike with a recovery tail for hard effort, a slow multi-hour lift for caffeine, a sustained trough for sleep. Maxima is the curve's peak, Minima its lowest point. It is a model for intuition, tuned to be plausible, not a measurement.

09

Pulling a pulse out of the noise

Synthetic signal, the engineer's bench

Reading an optical pulse at the temple means reading it through skin, scalp and the jaw muscle. Clinicians who pushed back named the reason directly: the true cardiovascular wave gets buried under slow scalp-flush drift and sharp jaw-clench bursts. This is a bench for pulling the pulse back out of that mess. The signal here is synthetic so the truth is known, but the filters are the real tools an engineer would reach for.

Raw optical signal corrupted·· bpm
Recovered pulse after the bench·· bpm

Filter bench

High-pass cutoff0.50 Hz
Removes the slow scalp-flush wander. Too high and it starts eating the pulse itself.
Burst rejection60%
A short-separation style gate that detects jaw-clench spikes and rides over them instead of letting them dominate.
Smoothingmoderate
A gentle low-pass to settle what is left. Push it too far and real beats blur together.
Recovered rate error--
Signal quality--

True pulse is held at a known rate the whole time. With the noise on and the bench idle, the raw estimate chases the noise. The skill on show is the trade: clear the drift and the bursts without flattening the very wave you are trying to read. That trade is the actual hardware problem this kind of device has to win.

10

Audit the agreement yourself

Open tool · runs in your browser

Temple's headline is a correlation: r = 0.93 against a metabolic cart. Correlation is the wrong test for a device that has to replace a measurement, and it is the easiest number to dress up. The honest test is a Bland-Altman: how far apart are the two methods, on average and at the edges. Drop a reference stream and a device stream below, or load the worked example, and this computes the bias and the 95% limits of agreement for you. Nothing is uploaded anywhere.

Two streams in

Each file is a CSV with a time column and one numeric column (heart rate, VO2, or any paired measure). The reference is the trusted instrument; the test is the device under question.

⇪
Click to choose, or drop two CSVs
reference first, then device. time, value per row.
Paired points
··
Bias (mean diff)
··
Upper limit (+1.96 SD)
··
Lower limit (−1.96 SD)
··
Within ±5 of reference
··
Bland-Altman · difference vs meanr = ··

Load the example to see the chart. A tight band hugging zero means the device can stand in for the instrument. A wide band, or a band that drifts as the values climb, means the correlation was hiding the disagreement that matters.

11

Bring a lab's data to the model

Open formats · schema bridge

Optical brain and tissue data already exists, from functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS), in thousands of academic recordings, in the open SNIRF and .nirs formats. A device that wants scientific trust should meet that data where it lives. This bridge reads the channel table a lab exports from a SNIRF record and rewrites it into a clean Temple stream: source-detector pairs become temporal optode channels, wavelengths are kept, and a frame schema is emitted so a model can be tested on real clinical signals before a single new subject is run.

Source record

Drop a channel export (CSV or JSON) from a SNIRF or .nirs file, or load the example montage. Raw SNIRF is HDF5, so labs export the channel table; this maps that table, it does not claim to parse the binary itself.

☷
Click to choose, or drop a channel export
CSV or JSON. Or load the example.
temple.stream.v1 · converted··
Load the example montage to see a SNIRF record rewritten as a Temple stream.
12

The validation that would settle it

Roadmap · honest public status

A claim becomes science when it survives a known path: a registered protocol, collected data, a pre-print, peer review, then independent replication. This tracks the studies that would actually validate Temple's claims against where each one publicly stands today. It is not a record of trials in progress; where the public status is empty, it says so. The point is to make the gap legible, the same way a reviewer would.

Open to critique · last reviewed 26 June 2026 · spotted an error or have a counter-argument? Tell me and I will address it here.

2

3

claimed, not public

none yet

Entropy test-retest reliabilityDoes the same person get the same number on a repeat day
expected next

Entropy beats existing load metricsAdds signal over heart rate, HRV and VO2, not just restates them
expected next
Protocol stage
Rest metabolism predicts lifespan in peopleThe cross-species thesis, tested in a human cohort with controls
none yet
Hypothesis only
Upright posture drives long-term declineA causal link from orthostatic flow to ageing, not just an acute dip
none yet
Hypothesis only

Status reflects what is publicly verifiable as of mid-2026. A single internal correlation is a start, not a result; the bar that matters is a registered protocol and an independent replication. This board is built to be updated the day any of that goes public.

Evidence scorecard

Three claims, scored on the evidence.

Not "true / false", strength of public support, the way a reviewer would mark it. The needle is cold when a claim outruns its evidence, gold when the evidence is there.

01 · SIGNAL

Optical pulse → energy

"Read metabolic demand at the temple."

Plausiblemethod sound · device unproven publicly
02 · LIFESPAN

Low rest = long life

"Across the animal world…"

Weakkernel survives in mammals · fails in birds
03 · GRAVITY

Gravity ages the brain

"Upright posture drives decline."

Speculativeacute effect real · causal leap unsupported
Why I built this

I wanted to show the work, not talk about it.

Temple is a genuinely interesting idea: read the body's demand at the temple, distil it into one living number, and use it to train and to age more slowly. I could not test their device. So I rebuilt the parts I could actually touch, in the open, with the data public and every figure sourced.

The point is not to grade them. It is to be the kind of teammate who can take an ambitious claim, build it honestly, and say plainly where it holds and where it breaks.

01Real data or labelled. Live datasets are computed here and reproducible. Illustrative signals say so.
02Agreement over correlation. A high r is the easiest number to win and to mislead with, so the bench tests for agreement.
03Sourced, not asserted. Every Temple fact is checked against a primary source.
04Flat results stay in. Where the data was boring or cut against me, that is the finding.
Methods & honesty

What's real, what's illustrative.

The fastest way to lose a serious reader is a hidden assumption. So here's the ledger for every panel, stated plainly.

Module
What's real vs illustrative
How to reproduce / extend
01 · Signal
synthetic signal real algorithm
Biquad bandpass, normalised‑LMS, radix‑2 FFT all run live. The PPG waveform is generated, not recorded.
Swap the generator for PPG‑DaLiA / WESAD windows; evaluate leave‑one‑subject‑out; report Bland‑Altman limits of agreement, not pooled r.
02 · Lifespan
real data real statistics
AnAge 2023, n=547. Raw, mass‑specific, multiple‑regression and per‑class partial correlations all computed from source.
Download AnAge from HAGR; OLS on log10 variables; regress log(lifespan) ~ log(mass) + log(MR). Script is provided alongside this page.
03 · Gravity
from literature
The autoregulation plateau and ~10 to 20% orthostatic CBF range are established physiology. No new measurements; the curves are representative, not a dataset.
Cross‑check against transcranial‑Doppler orthostatic studies, head‑up tilt and bed‑rest literature, and spaceflight (SANS) reviews.
Sources & verification

Every Temple claim here was checked against a primary source.

This only works if its own facts hold. As of June 2026 each load‑bearing claim below was verified against the source named beside it. Where Temple's own words and the press coverage diverged, the company's words win.

Entropy: r = 0.93 vs 0.55 against a metabolic cart, over 100 cardio sessionsDeepinder Goyal, X and LinkedIn, 19 Jun 2026 · no peer‑reviewed paper
Entropy is a live 1 to 250 index read at the superficial temporal arterytemple.com · launch post, Jun 2026
Credentialed scientific advisory board (Boas, Salat, Panda, Sandi, Posthuma and more)temple.com/advisors
$54M round, about $190M post‑money; Steadview, Peak XV, Info Edge, the Kamaths, Kunal Shah, VSSFeb 2026 · MCA/RoC filings via TechCrunch, Entrackr
The STA is an external‑carotid branch (scalp and face), disputed as a brain‑flow proxyAnatomy · neurologist Dr. Sudhir Kumar, 2026
Gravity Aging Hypothesis: about 10% upright CBF drop (press said up to 17%)continue.com hypothesis page, Oct 2025
Wearable energy expenditure is poorly validated (Fitbit Surge 27.4% to PulseOn 92.6% error)Shcherbina et al., J. Pers. Med., 2017
Real validation data: wrist PPG, accelerometer and chest ECG during exerciseJarchi & Casson 2017 · PhysioNet Wrist PPG During Exercise
Rate of living is largely rejected; birds out‑metabolise and outlive like‑sized mammalsde Magalhaes et al. 2007 · AnAge / HAGR
PPG‑in‑motion benchmarks: TROIKA ~2.4 bpm, JOSS ~1.3 bpm; daily life is harderZhang 2015; Temko 2015/2017; Reiss et al. (PPG‑DaLiA) 2019
Entropy Maxima and Minima: a peak that falls with age (push up) and a resting floor (lower is linked to longevity, bring down)Goyal, 19 Jun 2026 · Business Today
A second clinician, radiologist Dr. Suvrankar Datta (AIIMS), calls it "0 scientific standing" and names cfPWV the validated mortality markerDr. Suvrankar Datta on X, Jan 2026 · Business Standard, Business Today

Verified June 2026. Figures attributed to Temple are the company's public claims and remain unvalidated by peer review. This project neither endorses nor debunks them; it makes them checkable.
